questions.question: In a situation where a group of people can collectively benefit from a public good, but each person's contribution is difficult to monitor, which of the following is a likely outcome?
options: A) Everyone contributes the maximum amount
B) No one contributes, as there's no incentive
C) Some people contribute more than others
D) The number of contributors decreases over time

questions.question: A non-profit organization is working to build a new park in a local community. If each resident contributes a fixed amount of money to cover the costs, what is the optimal contribution amount per resident?
options: A) As low as possible
B) As high as possible
C) Just enough to cover the costs
D) More than enough to cover the costs

questions.question: In the context of public goods, what is the key difference between a 'lumpy' and 'smooth' good?
options: A) Lumpy goods are indivisible, while smooth goods are divisible
B) Lumpy goods are divisible, while smooth goods are indivisible
C) Lumpy goods are cheaper to produce, while smooth goods are more expensive
D) Lumpy goods are more popular, while smooth goods are less popular

questions.question: If a public good has a 'rivalrous' quality, what does this mean for its provision?
options: A) It can be easily replicated and shared
B) It cannot be replicated and shared
C) It can be easily stored and retrieved
D) It has no impact on the provision

questions.question: A government decides to provide a public good that has a 'non-rivalrous' quality. What is the most likely outcome?
options: A) Only a few people will use the good
B) Many people will use the good
C) The government will invest more in the good
D) The government will invest less in the good
